02 — Material Research  ·  Ongoing

Multanni Mitti

Started

GiR Programme
2022 — Ongoing

Material

Multanni Mitti
British Stoneware

Origin

Multan, Pakistan
& Scotland

Firing

Cone 9–10
Reduction

Multanni Mitti — Material Research, 2022–ongoing Replace with: img/multanni-hero.jpg

01 — The Material

What It Is

Multanni mitti is a fine-grained clay from the Multan region of Pakistan. It's been used for centuries — mainly in skincare, as a face pack and body treatment. People know it for that. What nobody seemed to have documented was what it does in a kiln at high temperatures, which is how it became a research project.

It's earthenware by nature. Its natural firing point is lower than stoneware, and when you push it past Cone 6 or up to Cone 9 and 10, the material behaves differently — it starts responding to glazes in ways the same glaze wouldn't produce over white stoneware. Reds and maroons appear where the glaze sits over the clay body. Crystal Emerald, in particular, reacts differently over multanni mitti — the copper carbonate picks up something from the clay underneath that I'm still tracking across firings.

Small pieces of Pakistan and Britain, linked together permanently in the kiln. As they already are through history.

It's used as a slip rather than a throwable clay body — applied as a stripe running along the waist of a pot before the glaze goes on. The stoneware provides the structure and the temperature tolerance. The multanni mitti does something to the surface that the stoneware alone won't.

Add image: slip application on leather-hard form
Add image: fired surface detail

02 — The Research

Testing &
Documenting

Every test piece gets logged — glaze recipe, layer count, thickness of the multanni mitti application, firing cone, atmosphere. It's the only way to actually understand what's changing between firings. Some variables are controlled, some aren't, and the ones that aren't often produce the most useful information.

The research started in earnest during the Graduate in Residence programme at Gray's in 2022. Initial firings confirmed the obvious — it fails at high temperatures if used as a primary clay body. The failures were the point. They defined the material's limits, which is what allowed the subsequent work to operate inside them.

The failures were the most informative firings. Knowing what it won't do is half the research.

Selected pieces from the early material study were first exhibited and sold at Crafted By Design in 2023. More recent work using the slip technique was shown at Cycle Arts Festival 2025. The research feeds directly into most of what's currently in production.

Add image: Multani Hover Pot
Add image: Slip Me A Large Sarge

03 — Firing Log

Observations So Far

Glaze

Crystal Emerald

Cone / Atmosphere

Cone 9 · Reduction

Observation

Copper carbonate reacting with clay body — reds and maroon tones appearing beneath the green. Not reproducible over white stoneware alone.

Glaze

White Bird Base

Cone / Atmosphere

Cone 09 · Reduction

Observation

Spangling behaviour amplified over multanni slip. Iron oxide spangles more pronounced. Surface character noticeably different from stoneware base.

Application

Cruden Bay Local Slip

Clay Body

White Stoneware · Multanni band

Observation

Scottish coastline slip alongside Pakistani clay body. Surface reads as layered history — both materials visible. Early iteration of the Hover Pot series.

Test Type

Primary Body Failure

Cone / Atmosphere

Cone 9–10 · Reduction

Observation

Earthenware clay body unable to tolerate high-fire temperatures as a structural material. Defined the limits that shaped all subsequent work — slip use only.

2022

Research Began

∞

Firings Still To Do

2+

Exhibitions Featuring This Work
